body_markdown:
> “We don’t want to be something for everybody,  
> we want to be everything for some people.”  
> — Carlos Ghosn

### **What Is This**

This lesson is about choosing your core client, it involves defining the specific group of clients you’ll target with your automated cold outreach. It doesn’t limit who you can sign as a client; it just helps focus your marketing efforts.

### Why Is This Important

-**Maximizes Value and Profitability:**

By focusing on a specific group that aligns with your unique skills and experiences, you can provide more value.  
-**Enhances Personalization:**

Automation works best when it feels personalized. Knowing your core client allows you to tailor your messages effectively.  
-**Improves Marketing Efficiency:**

Targeted efforts yield better results, saving time and resources.  
-**Helps Identify Pain Points:**

Understanding your core client's challenges allows you to address them more effectively.  
-**Builds Credibility:**

Using your relevant experience as proof boosts your credibility with potential clients.

### **When**

At the beginning of your business, especially before launching targeted automated marketing.

### **Who**

The coach needs to do this as it is a key decision based on their career, passions and any outside influences like non competes.

### **⌛ Time**

30 seconds to 3 days (only if really stuck)

## **How To Do It?** **This is Actually Really Simple**

For 19 out of 20 coaches this should be super simple and take about 10 seconds. You just fill in the industry/business type of this sentence where you have the most experience, expertise and can add the most value.

"My core client is {industry/business type] with 10-50 employees doing £1-£20 million revenue"

## Coaches

**Overcomplicate This,**

Don't Do That!

Think about the

**people and businesses you can add the most value to** because of your experience and choose them. For example, if you:

Experience

Core Client Business

Coach who did this...

Had your own engineering business

Engineering

Ashley Maile

Was MD at Manufacturing business (JCB)

Manufacturing /Engineering

James Baker

Got great results with a law firm client

Law firms

John McCarthy

Sales Director at HVAC Company

HVAC

Anne Melling

Built and sold a Drinks FMCG Brand

FMCG (Drinks, crisps, cookies)

Amanda Hamilton

Worked in Chemical industry 40 years

Chemical Companies

Eileen Lancaster

![duh-well.gif](https://cdn.disco.co/media/duh-well_532dd8b0-e82c-487e-a0f2-755a5ce1fe15.gif)

But it really is that simple!

# Every coach has clients outside of their core client industry!

This should hopefully help you relax about the decision. You as a business coach can coach any type of business. We are simply defining a group to improve the results for the automated Marketing, you are not limited in anyway who you work with. Let me repeat that...

# You Are Not Limited In Anyway Who You Can Work With!

* * *

### Why Do You Need A Core Client

When we come to run automated cold outreach there are a few critical parts to it being effective.

1.  **Personalised** - We want to be able to say "**Hi First Name, I see you run a UK Engineering Company**". This helps overcome that initial disinterest from them feeling they are being spammed. **It feels relevant to them.**

2.**Pain/Problem**- No one is ever going to pay you £2,000/mo if you aren't helping improve something in their business/life . Having a core client related to your experience helps you have a better understanding of what those problems and pain points are. So the second part of the message will be something like: "Are you looking to win bigger contracts" or "Are you looking to increase profits?" (Relevant to your core client).

3.**Proof -**

Once we have asked them about the pain or problem you are going to have to back it up with proof that you can help them. This is why we leverage your experience from your career or past clients. Below are examples from coaches messaging, this proof would come after identifying the prospect (e.g. I see you run a law firm) , then using a hook e.g. are you looking to increase profit), these all worked way better than KPI:**Eileen Lancaster (Career proof)**- "I ask because at Dow I used a simple system to increase Revenue from $2M → $40M in 5yrs (32% EBIT) by eliminating low volume accounts to focus on money makers."**James Baker (Career proof)**- I ask because as an MD at JCB I used a system to grow the Attachments Business from £150K/yr profit to £9M in 4yrs by keeping the team accountable.**John McCarty (Client proof)** - I ask because Stephen Ward, MD Clerksroom, grew his law firm’s profit from £200k to £1M in 3 years & cut his working hours in half in 1 year by following my PROFIT system

* * *

### The 5 Criteria for Picking Your Core Client

The proof is absolutely critical which is why of the 5 core criteria the #1 is value.

1.  **Most Value**- who can you provide and demonstrate the most value to given your skillset & experience. You know the pains, know what it feels like, you will empathise with them & ultimately create a better product/service/coaching that will provide more value.

2.**Pain**- is the market in pain? Do they have a desperate need for closing the gap. A nice to have is not something people are going to take their money out and buy. Most businesses always have issues they need to solve. Are they starving for the thing they are looking for?

3.**Growing**- otherwise always fighting the trend

4.**Easy To Find**- if you are marketing & you can't find your prospects then you can't get your offer in front of them. Want to be able to easily locate them in LinkedIn sales navigator search or if you have to in associations, groups etc. They must be congregated in one place.

5.**Purchasing Power**- do they have the money. This is why you want to avoid startups and charities.

You ideally want client in the 1 - 20 million revenue range and 11-50 employees but this is not set in stone and you should not limit yourself to businesses just this size.**Companies even below 1 million in revenue will pay coaches +£3,000/mo (Ask Pam how she knows).**

* * *

### How To Make The Decision

So you want to make sure your core client hits all of the criteria with the highest weight being given to #1. There are two strategies:

**Most Impressive**

What is most impressive from your career and background and then pick the person who would be most impressed by it as your core client. For example if you ran your own restaurant business for 10 years but then had to shut it down, but then grew a division of a major manufacturing company from £4M to £40M in 3 years well it's pretty clear you should pick the second one and manufacturing as your core client (Example from One of our coaches).**Most Confident**

If your whole family was going to die unless you massively improved the profit and performance of a business and you could choose that one business, what type of business would you choose. Would it be an airline? Would be a bakery? So if it's no to either of those then you can try and find what it is yes to or pick a bunch more businesses and keep eliminating No until you are left with the ones you have the most confidence with.

### **"I realised I had greater value especially where my focused market place was"**

* * *

### Special Considerations

Usually there are two categories of special circumstances where your core client has to be slightly adjust from the best proof you have.

1.  **Non Competes and Mitigating circumstances**

If you are not able to target that particular type of customer because of contacts, contracts and rules then you need to pick a different core client. The best thing to do here is to figure out what businesses/industries have similar pain points to the industry you have proof in and target that similar industry. For example if you can't target care homes, you could target nurseries (Also an example of a coach).

2.**Big Business!**  
Some industries are just prone to large companies e.g. banking, pharma, airlines. In this case you probably aren't going to start coaching the CEO of one of these companies so there are two options.

3.  1.  You target the MDs of divisions within these large companies as they have "ownership" over that department. One of Pam's longest standing clients was the MD of the Fishing Division of a multi billion import /exporter.
        
    2.  You find the smaller versions of the companies that your proof and experience would still be relevant to. See the Alternative Businesses For Large Scale Industries table at the end of this page for examples.
        

* * *

### Mistakes To Avoid

There are several traps unwary coaches fall into when picking a Core Client. You need to avoid these if you don’t want to waste time and effort.

**1 | Fear of Missing Out**

When coaches first think about a core client, they often fear missing out. Because picking a core client/niching your marketing down to a small part of the market means you’re excluding a massive number of the businesses you could work with. But you’ve got two choices.

1.  Be a generic coach who is trying to appeal to “anyone” and "everyone". And in the process, devaluing your skills and experiences  

    2.**Laser focus your marketing towards a specific set of decision-makers who can see why your skills and experiences mean you are their best chance of overcoming a problem or situation. And are prepared to pay you handsomely because of it.** **2 | Chasing The Money**

The second trap coaches fall into, is picking a niche they believe is going to be highly profitable when they don’t have experience in the niche or there’s no obvious link to why they should be taken seriously in that market.**Chasing a profitable niche where you have no experience will make your life far harder than it needs to be. As most prospects will think “you can’t help me as you don’t understand me”.**

Instead, serve a market you understand and have proven experience in. Or one where your skills are transferable in a way that makes it obvious why you should be paid well for your guidance.**3 | Fear of Making A Decision**

The third and most common trap is NOT making a decision for fear of making the wrong choice. Picking a niche is not a once in the lifetime decision. If you pick a Core Client and then realise it’s not for you, just change. And picking a Core Client type to focus on, doesn’t stop you working with anyone else if they directly ask for your help or are recommended to you. Instead of worrying between niche 1 or 2

**realise that by NOT choosing and not having focus you are actually choosing option 3** of not choosing, which is the only way to make a wrong decision.

#### **4 | Choosing an unfindable or non shared pain Core Client**

You have to be able to get them in a list and they have to have shared pain points. For example it's virtually impossible to find a group of family run businesses and they likely don't share the same issues when they are in different industries. Another example is a group like Military veteran business owners you can probably find but once again they will not have shared pain points so your marketing will have to be world class to connect with them over the military link.**5 | Changing Too Often**

Do not change your niche at the drop of the hat. If you keep changing, people will become confused and unsure as to what you do. Use this process to pick a suitable niche. When you become the best at serving clients is when you start to see the results. if you are changing every few weeks you can’t become the best. Go all out on it for at least six months.**Then, if it’s not for you, change it.** **6 | Choosing Clients Who Can't Pay**

While it's noble to want to help charities or startups, it's crucial to focus on clients who have the financial means to afford your services. Working with clients who can't pay can drain your resources and limit your ability to grow your business. Ensure that your Core Clients can not only benefit from your expertise but also compensate you fairly for your time and effort. Even though it feels like you can help them, your fees will put a lot of burden on their cashflow and within 3-4 months they won't be able to continue. Get yourself to 10-20k/mo first and then if you want to help them to give back you can.

## Conclusion

You could help any business. However it's much better when you come to run automation to have a targeted list.

Do not over think this! Choose the best proof from your career and the type of industry/business it is in choose that.

If there are special circumstances choose either an adjacent industry or a smaller similar type of firm.

* * *

### **FAQs**

**What if I choose the wrong core client?**

Choosing a core client isn't a lifelong commitment. It's important to make a decision based on current insights and be open to pivoting as you gain more data and experience.**How do I know if my core client is profitable?**

Validate the core client by ensuring it meets the key criteria (Who you can add value to, Pain, Growing, Easy to Find, Purchasing Power) and through initial marketing tests to gauge interest and willingness to pay. You can make virtually any market work but it takes focus and commitment.

* * *

### Alternative Businesses For Large Scale Industries

**Industry**

**Large Corporations**

**Alternative**

**Software Companies**

Major software firms, global tech giants

SaaS startups, local software development agencies, niche tech firms

**Finance**

Major banks, global investment firms, and large insurance companies

Boutique financial advisory firms, small investment firms, fintech startups

**Pharmaceuticals**

Big pharma companies, multinational biotechs

Local pharmacies, medical supply companies, small biotech startups

**Airlines**

Major airlines, large aviation companies

Private jet services, small regional airlines, aviation maintenance companies

**Energy and Utilities**

Major energy providers, utility companies, oil and gas conglomerates

Renewable energy startups, local utility cooperatives, energy consultancy firms

**Automotive Manufacturing**

Major automakers, large automotive parts suppliers

Auto repair shops, car dealerships, specialized parts manufacturers

**Telecommunications**

Major telecom providers, global network infrastructure companies

Local ISPs, telecom equipment resellers, niche telecom service providers

**Heavy Manufacturing**

Industrial machinery manufacturers, large-scale plants

Smaller manufacturing firms, custom fabrication shops, local production facilities

**Mining and Metals**

Large mining operations, metal production firms

Small mining exploration firms, metal recycling businesses

**Aerospace and Defence**

Major aerospace manufacturers, defence contractors

Aerospace component suppliers, defence tech startups, niche engineering firms

**Retail Giants**

Global retail chains, shopping conglomerates, large e-commerce platforms

Smaller retail stores, online boutique shops, local e-commerce businesses

**Media and Entertainment**

Major film studios, global TV networks, large media firms

Independent production companies, local TV/radio stations, digital content creators

**Real Estate**

Major developers, multinational property management firms

Local real estate agencies, boutique property management firms

**Banking and Insurance**

Major banks, global insurance companies, large financial institutions

Community banks, credit unions, local insurance agencies, fintech startups

This table should provide a clear overview to help identify suitable alternatives within traditionally large-scale industries.

### What Is This?

This lesson will teach you how to set the right pricing strategies for your coaching packages, ensuring that you get the best value for your services while meeting the needs of your clients.

### Why Is It Important/Useful?

-   **Optimize Revenue**: Proper pricing can maximize your revenue and profitability.
-   **Client Commitment**: Different pricing strategies can affect client commitment and retention.
-   **Cash Flow Management**: Upfront payments improve cash flow management by reducing financial uncertainty.
-   **Value Perception**: Clients often associate higher prices with higher value, which can enhance your brand reputation.
-   **Flexibility**: Offering various payment plans can accommodate different financial situations, making your services more accessible.

## How To Do It

## **Step 1: Understand Your Value**

Before setting any prices, understand the value you provide to your clients by watching . Think about:

-   The transformation and outcomes your clients can expect.
-   Your qualifications and experience.
-   Unique methodologies or tools you offer.**Nothing Is Set In Stone**

Some coaches with their experience and industry have immediately started charging more than £5,000/mo. Others have combined their coaching with consulting work for the 1st 3 months and then rolled them into pure coaching after that charging £15,000/mo for those 1st 3 months.

## **Step 2: Choose Your Pricing Models**

There are really two primary way to position your prices. You are selling the same thing it is just how you package it.

### **Model 1: Monthly Packages**

**Example:**

Starting at £1,450/mo

1.**Starter Price**: Begin by setting your monthly rate at £1,450 (higher if you wish but we found this is a good starting point).  

2. **Price Increases**: Plan to increase your price by £500 every 2-4 clients you bring on so £1,950/mo, £2,450/mo etc.

**Client Justification**: Understand that with this model, you'll need to justify the value delivered every month to retain clients.

### Model 2: 6-Month Packages

We think 6 months is a good length between it's not too much cash upfront but is enough time for people to see transformation. You can always try for 12 months but that's usually with clients you have worked with and built trust. If you have lots of authority which is what we show people how to build in our top programme these longer engagements can happen sooner.

**Example: Starting at £7,800 or £9,600 for 6 months**

1.**Upfront Payment Benefits**:  

2. - **Higher Cash Flow**: Get more cash upfront, improving financial stability.  
- **Reduced Monthly Justification**: You won’t need to justify your value each month; instead, focus on the overall transformation over 6 months.  
- **Value Perception**: Clients are likely to see a higher upfront cost as indicative of high value.

3.  **Payment Plans**:
4.  -   **Full Payment**: Offer the full 6-month package at £7,800 or £9,600.
    -   **2, 3 or 4 pay options:**

We have the payment plans mapped out for you in the sales training.  
        -**Monthly Payment Plan**: Equivalent to the monthly fee, providing flexibility and a consistent revenue stream.

**Which Is Better?**

Honestly the most appropriate answer is...It depends.

We don't have enough data to definitely say and I suspect it would vary massively by coach, client, industry, business stage, available cash. It also depends on personal preference and who you ask:**Pam -** prefers monthly because she is very confident in the value she adds and is excellent at retaining clients. She believes just get them signed up at the start and they'll stay for years and they don't need to keep thinking about it.**Zander**- prefers upfront because he is used to scaling through ads so cash upfront is important and likes that you can just staircase down with payment plans and end up at the monthly option anyway with the 6 pay. But he has also been used to selling online programmes that have a more defined end whereas Pam keeps clients 4, 5, 6 years (her longest was +9)**Alex Hormozi** - prefers as long as possible upfront because of what he calls "The Look Back Window". "**Customers determine whether a purchase was good based on the last purchase they made**" so if you do great the 1st month, terrible the second month they think about cancelling versus billing upfront they look at the overall improvement during that time. Watch video below (3min) for more detail...

## The Look Back Window

One way to think about making the decision of monthly or upfront

### **Step 3: Assess and Adjust**

Based on performance and feedback, be ready to adjust your pricing strategy. Incremental price increases every 2-4 clients can help you stay competitive while growing your revenue.

### **Step 4: Communicate Value**

Pricing is always relative to value.

-   If you offer massive value in excess of your pricing then

**you are leaving money on the table**.

-   If the value you deliver is only slightly more than the price then **you will have a hard time making sales.**

The numbers we have shared in this lesson are what works well for most coaches and business owners but always taking into consideration your industry, market, experience etc. We will show you exactly how to position your price in the sales training and we have some responses further down in this lesson if people ask you early in the process.

### **Step 5: Handle Objections**

We will show you how to handle price objections in the sales training but remember from the key is in defining the value you add, which we will also show you exactly how to do in the sales training.

# **Price Examples** **Sharing these to give you confidence that people do pay these figures for coaching.**## £4,950 + 6 x £995 = £11K for 6 months

Anne followed the sales process and sold a 6-month coaching package on a slightly different payment plan option 50% upfront front. So it was £4,950 upfront, followed by £995/mo. This approach not only ensures commitment but also provides immediate cash flow.

## £1,450/mo, £1,450/mo, £1,950/mo

James signed 3 clients in his 1st 3 weeks at the recommended price raising his prices on the 3rd client.

## 2 x £1,450/mo Clients In One Day

As John says in the video that's

**easily £50,000** in coaching fees coming his way.

## £600, £1,200, £1,500, £2,000

Ashley shares his price increases with encouragement from Pam asking "why are you not charging £2,000?"

## What To Say If Someone Asks You The Price

**BEFORE Sales Call & Demonstrated Value**

If someone asks you early on in the marketing process then

**you don't want to avoid the question**. Otherwise that makes them feel like you are hiding something. However, you also don't want to give a fixed amount before demonstrating value so we recommend saying this:

> "Thanks for asking, our/my Top coaching clients investment up to £50,000 a year but you can start for under £1,000/mo, it depends on your situation and what makes sense. I could do a call on (give two days e.g. Monday or Wednesday) to look at what makes sense for you. Which day works best for you?

The reason we do it like this is:

1.  You don't want to be evasive and avoid the question
2.  We want to anchor a very high price in their mind at the start so what we say next seems less
3.  We then want to alleviate concerns that they can't get started. Even if you minimum fee is £2,000/mo I'd say the £1,000 and at the end of the call if they remember what you said just say that was for once per month coaching.
4.  You want to take the lead and assume it makes sense to have the call and then just ask which day works best presumes it, they are more likely to just pick one.

**What You Say At The End of The Sales Call**>**For The BOSS Coaching that gets you to X/year revenue....(then choose either or)**>

> 1.**It's an upfront investment for the 1st 6 months of £9,600.**>**2. It's an monthly investment of £1,450 per month.**>  
> **And for that you get...(then we list out what they get)**

You will learn exactly how to do this in the training.

## Conclusion

Choosing the right pricing strategy is crucial for maximizing your revenue, enhancing client commitment, and improving cash flow. Whether you opt for monthly packages or a 6-month upfront plan, ensure that your pricing reflects the high value you offer. Track and adjust your pricing as needed, and always communicate the transformative impact your coaching can have on your clients’ businesses.

By implementing these pricing strategies, you're not just setting a price but building a sustainable and thriving coaching business.

### What Is This?

This guide explains how to structure your coaching offer effectively. It emphasizes selling outcomes rather than hours and suggests strategies to make your services more appealing and easier to manage.

### Why Is This Important?

Structuring your offer correctly will help you:

-   Give you clarity on what you are offering
-   Communicate value beyond just time spent.
-   Make it difficult for clients to calculate an hourly rate.
-   Focus on delivering measurable outcomes.
-   Manage your time efficiently by stacking sessions.

* * *

## How To Structure Your Coaching Offer

### Focus on Outcomes, Not Hours

**Avoid Hourly Rates:**

Selling coaching by the hour commoditizes your time and limits your earning potential. Instead, focus on the outcomes you help clients achieve.**Package-Based Approach:**

 We recommend positioning coaching for the long-term that emphasises the results clients will get, and they pay in 12, 6, 3 month packages in advance or ongoing monthly coaching. Always focused on bridging the gap talked about in . We setup the coaching structure in a way making it harder for them to compare your services based on hourly rates.

* * *

### Best Frequency for Coaching

Should you be doing it weekly, monthly, quarterly?

**Weekly sessions** can be too intense, giving clients little time to implement changes.**Monthly sessions** are too far apart, offering only 12 opportunities per year to course-correct.**2 x Per Month** strikes the perfect balance, providing ample time for action and reflection. However, during the first 4-6 weeks, weekly sessions are ideal to achieve quick wins and build momentum.

### Ideal Session Structure

**2 x 90-Minute Sessions Per Month:**

 This frequency makes it harder for clients to calculate an hourly rate and keeps them engaged without overwhelming them.

You do not have to do it this way, for example Ashley Maile does 1 x half day per month with a short call in between. He found that worked better for his engineering clients. So he just combined the two slots together.

* * *

### Stack Clients for Efficiency

**Blocking similar tasks together** is a time management strategy that enhances productivity. By stacking all clients on the 1st and 3rd Tue or Wed of each month, you manage your time effectively and create a predictable routine. This approach leaves other days free for marketing activities or enjoying your lifestyle; it even allows for taking two-week holidays without affecting client sessions.

If you're still in a job, you can start by stacking sessions on Saturday mornings of when your calendar allows.

While not essential, this practice is incredibly useful for maintaining a balanced and efficient schedule.

## **Example £10,000/mo Coach Calendar**

There aren't many other business that offer that income and that lifestyle

## What A Coaching Day Can Look Like

![CleanShot 2024-08-12 at 12.24.00@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2024-08-12_at_12_24_00_2x_349e68bb-ac4f-4272-b5a5-cc88c1f4a452.png)**John McCarthy explaining how he applies this exact concept of chunking clients on same days.**

* * *

### The Approach To Working With Clients:

# "As long as you are in business we are going to be working together."

At the start of the marketing and sales process you will focus on alleviating immediate problems and the pain those problems are creating, but during the sales call and in the first sessions you will always paint the long-term picture of working together. This helps retention.

### Quick Wins for Early Engagement

**First 4-6 Weeks:**

Work with clients

**weekly** to achieve some quick wins. This initial period is critical for building momentum and demonstrating the value of your coaching. It helps clients implement immediate changes and see early results. Keeps clients motivated and committed to the program.

### Ongoing Coaching

Transition into ongoing coaching with a balance of long-term plan and short term execution on a 2 x per month sessions.

Some coaches like to sell A 6-month package upfront. This timeline is long enough to achieve significant results but short enough to maintain client engagement. At the end of 6 months, the coach sells another 6-month package or switches to ongoing monthly payments. Some coaches prefer just ongoing monthly from the start.

We will cover prices and packages in more detail next:

* * *

### Summary

By structuring your coaching offer around outcomes rather than hours, you create a more compelling and valuable proposition for your clients. Using a package-based approach with a focus on achieving specific results within a set period can help you maximize your revenue and manage your time more effectively.

Start the coaching weekly for the first 4-6 weeks to gain quick wins and build momentum, then transition into 2 x 90 per month coaching with the option.

We will cover how you sell it and the specifics of the coaching framework to make it tangible later in the programme.

### What Is This?

This section explains what business owners are truly purchasing when they hire a coach. It's not just about the coaching itself; it's about bridging the gap between their current and ideal business.

### Why Is This Important?

Understanding what clients are actually buying will help you:

-   Communicate your value more effectively.
-   Tailor your services to meet their real needs.
-   Close sales more efficiently by addressing their pain points.
-   Create long-term client relationships by focusing on their ultimate goals.

By truly understanding this concept it answers 1000 other decisions you'll make when growing your coaching business.

* * *

# Understanding **What Clients Are Really Buying**## The 1️⃣ 2️⃣ 3️⃣ of Why Clients Buy

### **One Concept: The Gap**

Clients aren’t buying coaching services; they’re buying a solution to bridge the gap between their current business and their ideal business. Your role is to provide the knowledge, accountability, and outside perspective needed to close this gap.**What Does Bridging the Gap Mean in Practice?**

Below are examples of the improvements haps business owners may want broken down by the the original 6 pillars of the PROFIT System.**They Aren't Buying All Of Them...Yet**

When you market to a client they may only have 1 maybe 2 of these in mind, but over time this is what you will improve in the business

### Profit & Cash

-**Improved profitability:**

Helping them increase their profit margins and overall revenue.  
-**Cost reduction:**

Identifying opportunities to reduce expenses, such as negotiating better deals with suppliers.  
-**Better financial control:**

Providing support in budgeting, financial forecasting, and managing cash flow.

### Revenue & Marketing

-**Quality leads:**

Generating higher-quality leads to fuel sustainable business growth.  
-**Enhanced business strategy:**

Crafting strategies that align with their long-term vision and goals.  
-**Market penetration:**

Assisting in expanding their presence within existing markets or entering new markets effectively.

### Ops & Product

-**Customer retention:**

Developing strategies to improve customer satisfaction and loyalty.  
-**Operational efficiency:**

Identifying and eliminating bottlenecks to ensure smoother day-to-day operations.  
-**Improved supply chain:**

Optimizing supply chain management to reduce costs and improve delivery times.  
-**Enhanced productivity:**

Training team members on best practices and processes.

### Financials & KPIs

-**Clear KPIs:**

Setting clear financial and non-financial KPIs to measure the success of the business.  
-**Data-driven decisions:**

Using data and analytics to make informed decisions about the business direction.  
-**Regular monitoring:**

Setting up systems to monitor and report on KPIs regularly.  
-**Risk management:**

Implementing measures to mitigate business risks.

### Innovation & Management

-**Better team performance:**

Assisting in building and managing a more effective and cohesive team.  
-**Culture of innovation:**

Fostering a culture of continuous learning and improvement.  
-**Professional development:**

Providing opportunities for professional growth and training.  
-**Supportive environment:**

Creating a supportive and collaborative work environment.

### Team & Leadership

-**High-performing team:**

Investing in employee well-being and engagement.  
-**Team building:**

Providing opportunities for team building and collaboration.  
-**Positive work environment:**

Fostering a positive and inclusive work culture.  
-**Open communication:**

 Communicating openly and transparently with team members.

By addressing these aspects, you help clients move closer to their ideal business, making your coaching services invaluable.

![CleanShot 2024-07-20 at 16.09.09@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2024-07-20_at_16_09_09_2x_813481c2-1228-4d83-8725-f5e27a9076b7.png)

* * *

### **Two Dimensions of Improvement**

The world's economy runs on people closing the gap. The 4 main categories are health, wealth, relationships and happiness. However, when it comes to being a business coach it's best to think about it in 2 dimensions of closing the gap:

1.**Business Performance:**

2. - This includes metrics like profit, revenue, lead generation, system efficiencies, and team performance.  
- Clients want tangible improvements in these areas.

3.**Individual Well-Being/Personal Performance:**

4. - Clients also seek to improve their own skills, mindset, and productivity & lifestyle - e.g. if you get them home for dinner with their kids (and that's important to them) that is huge value.  
- A better-performing business often depends on the personal growth of the business owner. Also sometimes it has been affecting their health.

Different coaches will gravitate towards a different focus (and if can even vary on a client by client basis). For example:**Business Focus**- Our Global Coach of the year 2020-2022 Ashley Maile is very focused on driving the business and even says he isn't a great coach but an excellent mentor. He drives the business performance and wants clients who aren't interested in work life balance at this stage.**Individual Focus**- Whereas John McCarthy of 2023 Global Coach of The Year even though he still helps clients build incredible businesses most of his transformations talk about the health of his clients, them cutting down work hours and other aspects of getting their lifestyle back.

## **There is no right or wrong. You just want to understand how you add value.**

![CleanShot 2024-07-20 at 15.48.34@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2024-07-20_at_15_48_34_2x_15ea37fd-c93c-4ea9-8271-c7ead68b0735.png)

* * *

### **Three Key Reasons Clients Will Buy Your Coaching**

But why do people now just close this gap on their own? Many reasons but it usually comes down to:

1.**Lack Ability:**

They don’t know how to reach their goals on their own.  
2.**Speed of Achievement:**

They believe they can achieve their goals faster with your help.  
3.**Ease and Stress Reduction:** They feel it will be less stressful and easier with a coach guiding them.

It can either be one reason or multiple.

![CleanShot 2024-07-20 at 16.43.29@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2024-07-20_at_16_43_29_2x_b96dad9f-49d6-420b-85f2-5652ab75440b.png)

* * *

### Personal Trainer Analogy

Think of it like hiring a personal trainer. People looking to lose weight aren’t interested in hiring a personal trainer; they’re interested in bridging the gap between their current body and their ideal body. Here’s how it plays out with the 1-2-3 framework:

**1. The Gap:** **Current Body vs. Ideal Body:**

Just like business owners want to transform their current business into their ideal business, individuals want to transform their current physique into their ideal body.**2. The Two Dimensions of Improvement:**

1.**Nutrition:**

2. - Knowing what to eat and when to eat it.  
- Understanding macronutrient ratios and caloric intake.

3.**Exercise:**

4. - Knowing which exercises to perform for maximum efficiency.  
- Understanding the correct form and technique.**3. The Three Key Reasons People Hire Personal Trainers:**

1.**Lack of Knowledge:**

They don’t know the right workout routines or what to eat. Similar to business owners who don’t know how to improve their business on their own. What should they focus on?

2.**Speed of Achievement:**

Correcting exercises and optimizing workout routines help them achieve results faster. Just like a coach helps business owners reach their goals more quickly.

3.**Ease and Stress Reduction:** Having someone to keep them accountable makes it easier to stick to their gym routines. Similarly, business owners find it easier to implement strategies and maintain focus with a coach’s guidance as long as the coach is challenging them and not allowing them to get pulled into the day to day putting out the fires.

* * *

### Conclusion

Understanding this will help you communicate effectively with your clients, highlighting the real value of your coaching services. Remember, it's about bridging "The Gap" between their current situation and their ideal business state. The secret to client acquisition is to position your coaching as a ‘bridge’ to overcome this gap. To sell well, your coaching must be a vehicle for change.

![CleanShot 2024-07-20 at 16.44.59@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2024-07-20_at_16_44_59_2x_ccdc29ca-0378-4370-a366-b9ee64ca436a.png)

This is why you must never ever, ever, ever, ever say things like:

-   "Would you be interested in coaching"
-   "Shall we setup a call to see how my system can help you"
-   "Would be great to discuss how we work together." (Great for you, Not for them!)

# You are selling outcomes, futures, solutions.

## That's What They Are Buying,

So That's What You Must Be Selling...

So let's talk about how you structure what you sell...

## This Will Make You a More Confident Coach (Before you even sign a client)

Confidence is one of the things I hear coaches talk about most, whether you're just starting out or you've been doing this for years.

And here's what I've come to believe: confidence doesn't come from doing more sessions, getting more qualifications, or waiting until you feel ready. Confidence comes from clarity. Specifically, clarity about who you are as a coach.

When you know your archetype, when you can name the kind of coach you actually are rather than the kind you think you **should** be, something shifts. You stop second-guessing your instincts. You stop apologising for your style. You start owning it. And when you own it, your clients feel it.

That's what this post is about.

## Three Traits That Define Your Coaching Style

After working with coaches across this community, I've found there are three dimensions that shape how you show up in a session. None of these is better or worse than any other. They're just different. And knowing where you sit changes everything.**1. Mentor to Coach**(1 = pure mentor, 5 = pure coach)

A mentor draws on their own experience. They've walked the path, they know what worked, and they share it. There's real power in that. Business owners often desperately need someone who's been there to just tell them what to do.

A pure coach believes the answer is already inside the client. Their job is to ask the question that helps the client hear themselves think. They're not there to give the answer. They're there to make the question unavoidable.

Ashley sits firmly at the mentor end. He's built businesses, he knows what works, and he'll tell you clearly. Mark is at the other extreme. He'll rarely share his own view in a session. He'll ask you questions until you arrive at the answer yourself, and when you do, it sticks in a way that being told never quite does. Pam sits somewhere in the middle, blending both approaches depending on what the client needs in the moment.**2. Growth-Focused to Lifestyle-Focused**(1 = growth-first, 5 = lifestyle-first)

Some coaches are wired to help clients scale. Revenue, team, market share. When they look at a business, they see opportunity and they want to chase it. That energy is infectious and it moves people.

Others believe the business exists to serve the life, not the other way around. They think holistically about the owner's health, relationships, and fulfilment, not just the P&L. They'll push back when a client is winning in business but losing in life.

Ashley is a 1 here too. He is focused on growth and he makes no apology for it. He only takes on clients who genuinely want to scale their business. When someone comes to him wanting primarily lifestyle balance, he refers them on, because he knows another coach will serve them far better. Pam sits at the other end of this scale, and there's a personal reason for that. She built a business that nearly cost her everything, her health, her relationships, the very life she was building the business for. That experience shaped her deeply. She still wants her clients to grow. But she will never let them lose sight of why they're building it. The business should serve the life. When it starts consuming the life instead, Pam will name it.**3. Tough Love to Nurturing**(1 = tough love, 5 = nurturing)

Some coaches challenge hard. They hold clients accountable with very little softening, call out the stories clients tell themselves, and create pressure that produces results. Clients who need a push thrive with them. Imagine Gordon Ramsay feedback but a little less swearing!

Others lead with warmth. They create safety, they encourage, they meet the client exactly where they are, and from that place of trust, remarkable things happen. Clients who need permission to believe in themselves thrive with them.

Ashley brings tough love. He will hold your feet to the fire and he won't dress it up. I don't know for certain but going on personalities imagine people like James Baker leads with genuine warmth, challenging clients but encouraging. He creates a space where clients feel safe enough to be truly honest, and then he uses that honesty to move them forward. Both get extraordinary results. The difference is the environment they create to get there.

### So what is your style?

Below I created a little tool for you to play with to see the different coaches and their style and you can think about how you may compare. Click on a coaches name to apply what i believe is their score on the scales.

## Why This Matters for Your Confidence

Here's what happens when you don't know your archetype: you try to be everything. You're tough when you think you should be tough and warm when you think you should be warm, and neither feels natural because you're performing a version of coaching rather than actually being a coach.

Here's what happens when you do know: you stop performing. You make decisions in the session from instinct rather than anxiety. A client pushes back and instead of wobbling, you hold your position, because you know why you do what you do.

Look at Ashley. He is completely clear on who he is and who he serves. That clarity means he never wastes energy trying to be something he's not. Look at Pam. Her experience gives her a perspective that no qualification could teach, and knowing that is part of what makes her so effective. Look at Mark. He trusts the process of pure coaching so completely that clients trust it too, even when it feels uncomfortable. That trust comes from his clarity about who he is.

Knowing your archetype also means knowing your ideal client. When you're clear on your style, you naturally attract the people who will thrive with you, and you can refer on the people who won't, with confidence rather than guilt.**If you try to appeal to everyone you appeal to no one!**## We Want to Hear From You

Drop your scores in the comments below. Give yourself a number from 1 to 5 on each of the three traits:**Mentor to Coach**

— 1 means "I draw on my experience and tell people what to do." 5 means "I rarely share my own view. I draw everything out of the client."**Growth to Lifestyle**

— 1 means "I'm focused on scaling the business above all else." 5 means "I think holistically about life and business together."**Tough Love to Nurturing** — 1 means "I challenge hard and don't soften much." 5 means "I lead with warmth, encouragement, and emotional safety."

Your comment might look something like this: "I'm a 2-4-3. I lean mentor, I think holistically, and I'm somewhere in the middle on tough vs warm."

One thing I'd ask: be honest rather than aspirational. The goal isn't to post the score that sounds most impressive. It's to name what's actually true. That's where the clarity comes from. And that's where the confidence follows.

I'd love to see how we're all spread across the scale. I suspect it'll spark a really good conversation.

### **What Is This**

This lesson is about choosing your core client, it involves defining the specific group of clients you’ll target with your automated cold outreach. It doesn’t limit who you can sign as a client; it just helps focus your marketing efforts.

### Why Is This Important

-**Maximizes Value and Profitability:**

By focusing on a specific group that aligns with your unique skills and experiences, you can provide more value.  
-**Enhances Personalization:**

Automation works best when it feels personalized. Knowing your core client allows you to tailor your messages effectively.  
-**Improves Marketing Efficiency:**

Targeted efforts yield better results, saving time and resources.  
-**Helps Identify Pain Points:**

Understanding your core client's challenges allows you to address them more effectively.  
-**Builds Credibility:**

Using your relevant experience as proof boosts your credibility with potential clients.

### **When**

At the beginning of your business, especially before launching targeted automated marketing.

### **Who**

The coach needs to do this as it is a key decision based on their career, passions and any outside influences like non competes.

### **⌛ Time**

30 seconds to 3 days (only if really stuck)

## **How To Do It?** **This is Actually Really Simple**

For 19 out of 20 coaches this should be super simple and take about 10 seconds. You just fill in the industry/business type of this sentence where you have the most experience, expertise and can add the most value.

"My core client is {industry/business type] with 10-50 employees doing £1-£20 million revenue"

# Every coach has clients outside of their core client industry!

This should hopefully help you relax about the decision. You as a business coach can coach any type of business. We are simply defining a group to improve the results for the automated Marketing, you are not limited in anyway who you work with. Let me repeat that...

# You Are Not Limited In Anyway Who You Can Work With!

* * *

### Why Do You Need A Core Client

When we come to run automated cold outreach there are a few critical parts to it being effective.

1.  **Personalised** - We want to be able to say "**Hi First Name, I see you run a UK Engineering Company**". This helps overcome that initial disinterest from them feeling they are being spammed. **It feels relevant to them.**

2.**Pain/Problem**- No one is ever going to pay you £2,000/mo if you aren't helping improve something in their business/life . Having a core client related to your experience helps you have a better understanding of what those problems and pain points are. So the second part of the message will be something like: "Are you looking to win bigger contracts" or "Are you looking to increase profits?" (Relevant to your core client).

3.**Proof -**

Once we have asked them about the pain or problem you are going to have to back it up with proof that you can help them. This is why we leverage your experience from your career or past clients. Below are examples from coaches messaging, this proof would come after identifying the prospect (e.g. I see you run a law firm) , then using a hook e.g. are you looking to increase profit), these all worked way better than KPI:**Eileen Lancaster (Career proof)**- "I ask because at Dow I used a simple system to increase Revenue from $2M → $40M in 5yrs (32% EBIT) by eliminating low volume accounts to focus on money makers."**James Baker (Career proof)**- I ask because as an MD at JCB I used a system to grow the Attachments Business from £150K/yr profit to £9M in 4yrs by keeping the team accountable.**John McCarty (Client proof)** - I ask because Stephen Ward, MD Clerksroom, grew his law firm’s profit from £200k to £1M in 3 years & cut his working hours in half in 1 year by following my PROFIT system

* * *

### The 5 Criteria for Picking Your Core Client

The proof is absolutely critical which is why of the 5 core criteria the #1 is value.

1.  **Most Value**- who can you provide and demonstrate the most value to given your skillset & experience. You know the pains, know what it feels like, you will empathise with them & ultimately create a better product/service/coaching that will provide more value.

2.**Pain**- is the market in pain? Do they have a desperate need for closing the gap. A nice to have is not something people are going to take their money out and buy. Most businesses always have issues they need to solve. Are they starving for the thing they are looking for?

3.**Growing**- otherwise always fighting the trend

4.**Easy To Find**- if you are marketing & you can't find your prospects then you can't get your offer in front of them. Want to be able to easily locate them in LinkedIn sales navigator search or if you have to in associations, groups etc. They must be congregated in one place.

5.**Purchasing Power**- do they have the money. This is why you want to avoid startups and charities.

You ideally want client in the 1 - 20 million revenue range and 11-50 employees but this is not set in stone and you should not limit yourself to businesses just this size.**Companies even below 1 million in revenue will pay coaches +£3,000/mo (Ask Pam how she knows).**

* * *

### How To Make The Decision

So you want to make sure your core client hits all of the criteria with the highest weight being given to #1. There are two strategies:

**Most Impressive**

What is most impressive from your career and background and then pick the person who would be most impressed by it as your core client. For example if you ran your own restaurant business for 10 years but then had to shut it down, but then grew a division of a major manufacturing company from £4M to £40M in 3 years well it's pretty clear you should pick the second one and manufacturing as your core client (Example from One of our coaches).**Most Confident**

If your whole family was going to die unless you massively improved the profit and performance of a business and you could choose that one business, what type of business would you choose. Would it be an airline? Would be a bakery? So if it's no to either of those then you can try and find what it is yes to or pick a bunch more businesses and keep eliminating No until you are left with the ones you have the most confidence with.

### **"I realised I had greater value especially where my focused market place was"**

* * *

### Special Considerations

Usually there are two categories of special circumstances where your core client has to be slightly adjust from the best proof you have.

1.  **Non Competes and Mitigating circumstances**

If you are not able to target that particular type of customer because of contacts, contracts and rules then you need to pick a different core client. The best thing to do here is to figure out what businesses/industries have similar pain points to the industry you have proof in and target that similar industry. For example if you can't target care homes, you could target nurseries (Also an example of a coach).

2.**Big Business!**  
Some industries are just prone to large companies e.g. banking, pharma, airlines. In this case you probably aren't going to start coaching the CEO of one of these companies so there are two options.

3.  1.  You target the MDs of divisions within these large companies as they have "ownership" over that department. One of Pam's longest standing clients was the MD of the Fishing Division of a multi billion import /exporter.
        
    2.  You find the smaller versions of the companies that your proof and experience would still be relevant to. See the Alternative Businesses For Large Scale Industries table at the end of this page for examples.
        

* * *

### Mistakes To Avoid

There are several traps unwary coaches fall into when picking a Core Client. You need to avoid these if you don’t want to waste time and effort.

**1 | Fear of Missing Out**

When coaches first think about a core client, they often fear missing out. Because picking a core client/niching your marketing down to a small part of the market means you’re excluding a massive number of the businesses you could work with. But you’ve got two choices.

1.  Be a generic coach who is trying to appeal to “anyone” and "everyone". And in the process, devaluing your skills and experiences  

    2.**Laser focus your marketing towards a specific set of decision-makers who can see why your skills and experiences mean you are their best chance of overcoming a problem or situation. And are prepared to pay you handsomely because of it.** **2 | Chasing The Money**

The second trap coaches fall into, is picking a niche they believe is going to be highly profitable when they don’t have experience in the niche or there’s no obvious link to why they should be taken seriously in that market.**Chasing a profitable niche where you have no experience will make your life far harder than it needs to be. As most prospects will think “you can’t help me as you don’t understand me”.**

Instead, serve a market you understand and have proven experience in. Or one where your skills are transferable in a way that makes it obvious why you should be paid well for your guidance.**3 | Fear of Making A Decision**

The third and most common trap is NOT making a decision for fear of making the wrong choice. Picking a niche is not a once in the lifetime decision. If you pick a Core Client and then realise it’s not for you, just change. And picking a Core Client type to focus on, doesn’t stop you working with anyone else if they directly ask for your help or are recommended to you. Instead of worrying between niche 1 or 2

**realise that by NOT choosing and not having focus you are actually choosing option 3** of not choosing, which is the only way to make a wrong decision.

#### **4 | Choosing an unfindable or non shared pain Core Client**

You have to be able to get them in a list and they have to have shared pain points. For example it's virtually impossible to find a group of family run businesses and they likely don't share the same issues when they are in different industries. Another example is a group like Military veteran business owners you can probably find but once again they will not have shared pain points so your marketing will have to be world class to connect with them over the military link.**5 | Changing Too Often**

Do not change your niche at the drop of the hat. If you keep changing, people will become confused and unsure as to what you do. Use this process to pick a suitable niche. When you become the best at serving clients is when you start to see the results. if you are changing every few weeks you can’t become the best. Go all out on it for at least six months.**Then, if it’s not for you, change it.** **6 | Choosing Clients Who Can't Pay**

While it's noble to want to help charities or startups, it's crucial to focus on clients who have the financial means to afford your services. Working with clients who can't pay can drain your resources and limit your ability to grow your business. Ensure that your Core Clients can not only benefit from your expertise but also compensate you fairly for your time and effort. Even though it feels like you can help them, your fees will put a lot of burden on their cashflow and within 3-4 months they won't be able to continue. Get yourself to 10-20k/mo first and then if you want to help them to give back you can.

## Conclusion

You could help any business. However it's much better when you come to run automation to have a targeted list.

Do not over think this! Choose the best proof from your career and the type of industry/business it is in choose that.

If there are special circumstances choose either an adjacent industry or a smaller similar type of firm.

* * *

### **FAQs**

**What if I choose the wrong core client?**

Choosing a core client isn't a lifelong commitment. It's important to make a decision based on current insights and be open to pivoting as you gain more data and experience.**How do I know if my core client is profitable?**

Validate the core client by ensuring it meets the key criteria (Who you can add value to, Pain, Growing, Easy to Find, Purchasing Power) and through initial marketing tests to gauge interest and willingness to pay. You can make virtually any market work but it takes focus and commitment.

## **Purpose**

## To share with you the only two elements you MUST focus on to achieve results. Everything else is irrelevant to getting clients. By mastering these concepts and following this proven formula, you're setting yourself up for guaranteed success. This is so important because if you ever struggle to sign clients, the issue and solution is in this lesson.You cannot fail, if you follow these stepsIf you apply these strategies consistently, you cannot fail. This lesson will be your go-to guide whenever you need to refocus and ensure you're on the path to achieving your business goals.Introduction: The Simple Formula for SuccessSuccess in signing clients as a business coach can be boiled down to a simple formula: 

## Volume x Conversion = Results

Understanding and optimising these two factors will lead to increased business success. In fact it is the ONLY thing that can.**If you have struggled to achieve results** it's because one or BOTH of these were missing.**Understanding Volume and Conversion**

Let's break these two concepts down into their core definitions and then build a strategy from there.**Understanding Volume**

Volume is about how many people you reach out to with your coaching offer. This isn't about selling right away; it's about seeing if they're interested in solving a pain/problem they have. The more people you reach out to, the better your chances of finding potential clients. Volume has two key parts:

1.**Outreach Rate**: This is how many people you contact in a given time period, like daily or weekly. It's important to be aware of the outreach limits on platforms you are using. For example LinkedIn, you can only send 100-200 connection requests per week. You can't suddenly send 5,000 requests, so your outreach should align with these capacity limits, and this is why the next concept then becomes important...

2. 

**Consistency**: This means how regularly you keep up your outreach efforts. For example, if you reach out to potential clients every day, you're being consistent. So you want to be consistently maximising the outreach rate to the capacity available. I.e. sending 100-200 connection requests week after week (and other methods to increase capacity).

Together, these create your overall Volume:

## **Outreach Rate x Consistency = Volume**## By focusing on both how many people you reach out to and how often you do it, you can increase your chances of success in finding new clients. See image below as a representation of the difference.

![CleanShot 2025-01-06 at 09.37.42@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2025-01-06_at_09_37_42_2x_e226b393-fb4e-4448-80f6-f756744ee59c.png)

### Understanding Conversion

Conversion is the rate at which your outreach efforts turn into paying clients. For example, if you send out 1,000 connection requests on LinkedIn and gain one client, your conversion rate is 0.1%. This is a good target to aim for. Another coach might need to send 10,000 requests to get one client, resulting in a 0.01% conversion rate. Our goal is to achieve the highest conversion rate possible.**Different Levels of Conversion**

1.**Macro Conversion**: This is the big goal—turning a prospect into a paying client. It's the ultimate success metric for your outreach efforts.

2.  **Milestone Conversions**: These are the key steps along the way to a macro conversion. For example, moving an interested lead to a booked call, then to a discovery call, and finally to a sales call. Each milestone is important for guiding prospects through the sales journey.
    
3.  **Micro Conversions**: These are the small actions that lead to milestone conversions. For instance, for a prospect to become interested, they need to see and accept your connection request, then reply to express interest. For them to book a call, they need to read your message, click a link, and schedule the call.
    

Understanding these levels helps you see where you can improve each step of the process to boost your overall conversion rate.

#### ***What Influences Conversion?***#### 

#### **Conversion is influenced by two main factors:**

Pain and*** Confidence***. These are the driving forces that motivate a potential client to take action.

1.  **Pain**: This represents the problem or need your potential client is experiencing. The greater the discomfort or urgency they feel, the more likely they are to seek a solution.
    
2.  **Confidence**: This is the trust and belief that your potential client has in your ability to solve their problem. The more confidence they have in you and your offer, the more likely they are to take action.
    

You can think of it like this:

(Pain Level + Confidence Level) / 2 = Conversion (Likelihood of Action)

If a person's pain and confidence levels are high enough to cross their action threshold, they're more likely to become your client. By increasing the perceived pain of staying the same and boosting confidence in your solution, you enhance the chance of conversion.

### Visualising Conversion Steps

Below is a visual representation of the default conversion numbers, illustrating how outreach efforts transition through the stages of the Business Coach funnel. This visual helps clarify the pathway from reaching out to signing new clients.

![CleanShot 2025-01-05 at 12.44.04@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2025-01-05_at_12_44_04_2x_1f7314f0-3cbe-4f3f-9ea6-f419b715a634.png)

## Relating Volume and Conversion

Explore how volume and conversion rates work together to achieve your client goals. This section will illustrate how different approaches can lead to the same result, using real-life examples.**Case Study Comparisons**:

1.  -   **Ashley Maile's Approach**: With a deep understanding of his industry and strong credibility, Ashley achieved a high conversion rate. By speaking to only 6-10 prospects, he secured 6 clients, demonstrating how expertise and trust can minimize the need for high outreach volume.
        
    -   **John McCarthy's Strategy**: In a more skeptical industry, John needed to build credibility from the ground up. He sent out 6,000 outreaches to achieve a 0.1% conversion rate, ultimately securing 6 clients. This highlights how increasing volume can compensate for lower conversion rates.
        

**You can do both!**

 Number of Campaigns & KPIs Needed to Reach £10,000/month

The table below shows the KPIs you need to hit on your conversion part of your business and how many campaigns that will mean you need to achieve £10,000/mo coaching. This assumes you are ONLY signing clients through cold outreach which should not be the case. It also assumes 600 outreaches per campaign (300 in each A/B test variation)**Key**

### Now lets break down the 9 tests...

![CleanShot 2025-01-04 at 11.37.21@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2025-01-04_at_11_37_21_2x_3105754b-cc8e-4638-ad58-59b26610be1a.png)

### **Purpose** This is a core lesson of the Academy. Providing you a simplified roadmap to reach £10,000 per month as a Business Coach. It will give you the clarity by providing you with a structure pland (our curriculum of courses) so you can easily track your journey and know when to seek help. ### The £10,000/mo Business Coach The simplest way to 10K per month is 6 clients. We use $/£ interchangeably. - 4 clients at £1,500/mo - 2 clients at £2,000/mo ![6 clientspng.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/6_clientspng_80baf41a-0113-4bf3-82cf-195b6b7fb035.png) Some people who join the Academy can start charging much higher immediately based on their experience and sales skills. * * * ## Building Your Bridge To Success To help explain how The Business Coach Academy helps you achieve your goal we have this analogy. ![CleanShot 2024-11-15 at 16.45.03@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2024-11-15_at_16_45_03_2x_1f4cf743-4c16-42a3-84e1-5a1253c6d058.png) ## **The Three Pillars**## Pillar #1 - Campaigns (Businessville) ## You must run campaigns to the citizens of Businessville to see who is interested in moving to client city.**Pillar #2 - Conversion (Conversion Crossing)** Guide as many interested citizens as possible across Conversion Crossing. This bridge is where you transform interest into commitment through a discovery call (30m), sales calls (90m) and lead them toward becoming valued clients.**Pillar #3 - Client City (Coaching)** If your goal is 10K/mo then the aim is get 6 citizens into Client City, where you deliver transformation to the business performance and owners wellbeing through your coaching and retain them as clients.**Why Are These The Key Things To Focus On?** * * * ## The Path From £0 to £10K/mo By reverse engineering how you produce cashflow it shows why these 3 pillars are key: ![CleanShot 2024-11-15 at 17.57.12@2x.png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/CleanShot_2024-11-15_at_17_57_12_2x_b059f0bc-dd6b-4d27-8fce-1a48224dd7dc.png) We can break down Cashflow into the only 2 elements to improve it, as shown above: - **Get More Customers**: Increase the number of people who buy from you. - **Make Each Customer Worth More**: Enhance the value each customer brings. But we don't have to stop there. The 3 building blocks of those two things are: - **Marketing**- running  **campaigns**  to generate interested leads. -**Sales**- taking a lead through  **conversion**  steps to become a client -**Delivery**- giving exceptional  **coaching**  to produce results and keep clients longer. Bring it all together and you get the core parts for the **Value chain of the business**. The foundational elements: - **Pillar #1 - Campaigns**: Choosing the right avatar, optimising profiles and running lead generation campaigns. - **Pillar #2 - Conversion**: Optimising lead follow-up, lead nurture, and converting sales. - **Pillar #3 - Coaching**: Delivering transformation sessions, developing your skills and following a system. - **Core Offer**: Setting the right promise, packages and price for your services. ***Core offer is part of all three pillars.Key InsightIf you ever don't have the cashflow you want it's because you either: 1. don't have enough customers or 2. they are not worth enough. So you need to fix either the marketing, sales or deliver which ultimately leaves you needing to fix One of these four things: Campaigns, Conversion, Coaching or Core offer. ### The Great News Is... # The Academy Covers All of Those From End To End # **You just need to follow it in order!** * * * ## The Course Content of The Academy Below is the outline for the ProCoach Course Content. It shows the Main Pillars and then the courses and description. ![ProCoach Roadmap Graphics (1).png](https://cdn.disco.co/media/ProCoach_Roadmap_Graphics__1__a809327a-29f8-425d-8d35-c153935c68fd.png) ## Foundations The things you need to know before you get into the main roadmap (shown above). Get the basics of how to build a successful business. Learn how to use the tools in The Academy. Master your own time, energy, mindset to progress at lightening speed. ## Pillar #1: Campaigns You have to make your offer known to people through campaigns: You need to know who and which businesses are the right traffic to generate. You need to optimise your main online profile where you will be running traffic. And then you need to know the traffic strategies to generate leads. ## Pillar #2: Conversion The higher your conversion the less work you need for the same results: Once you have traffic coming in you need to properly follow-up to get short-term conversion. Not everyone will convert so you need to be able to continuously nurture to increase long-term conversion. It's vital you can convert prospects into paying clients through an efficient sales call process. ## Pillar #3: Coaching The better coach you are the easier it is to make money: First you need the coaching skills to understand how to help any business owner, in any situation. Then you need to be able to deliver high value sessions to your clients for quick wins. You need to be skilful at creating transformation so you can retain clients. ### All you need to do whenever you are stuck is... # Apply the content of the area you need to improve! * * * ## The Business Coach Academy Curriculum So now you understand why we have the core pillars and you have seen the courses within them. Below is a detailed view of the Academy course content. You can access the content by clicking "courses" in the left hand side bar or [**clicking here**](https://https://academy.businesscoachacademy.com/experiences/upcoming)**.** You can click the arrows to the left of the course names to expand it and see the lessons. Then you can also click on a lesson to access the resources. # Take action on the course content and you can become a 10K/mo high impact Business Coach in 6-12 months.
